text: Hi, this is Abu Bakr Ali. I'm presenting a case of a truncal vagotomy in Heineken-Mickles pyloriplasty with a misdiagnosis and a complication.
This is a patient with a complex surgical history. He was involved in a car accident.
He had a Missed a bowel injury that led to him undergoing an X-lap.
Abdomen was open for two weeks. It was closed again. He leaked again. Abdomen again open for two weeks.

He also had some sternal fixation, had an aortic transection requiring an aortic graft.

So, um, uh, about seven years later, he presented to the hospital with the, uh, uh, uh, with the perforated ulcer, uh, because the fear of him having all of these surgery, uh, he was treated kind of conservatively. He ended up with, uh,

gastric outlet obstruction requiring a PEG tube place and I got involved for an esophageal bad esophageal stricture initial was thought to be cancer turned out to be a peptic stricture and requiring multiple dilation

So I kind of felt the only way of getting him through is fixing his hiatal hernia and dealing with the gastric outlet obstruction. By performing Heineken-Mickle's pyloriplasty felt that the vagotomy will reduce the acid, fixing the hiatal hernia will also reduce the acid reflux.
As mentioned, he's a Jehovah Witness. It took about two hours just to kind of get to this, like to be able to kind of see the hiatus of laparoscopic lysovedesian.

So I started with, I dilated him probably about, Uh, six weeks prior to the surgery, uh, there was a mucosal tear, uh, indicating really good dilation, um, and just delayed him for six weeks and very aggressive PPI treatment.

The surgeon performs dissection of the hiatal hernia, followed by an anterior vagotomy. The posterior vagus nerve is challenging to locate, prompting the use of a Penrose drain for retraction and tension to aid in identifying the nerve band.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the posterior vagus nerve, confirming its presence alongside the anterior vagus nerve. Both nerves are sent for frozen section analysis to ensure accurate identification.

The surgeon identifies the posterior vagus nerve, which is located to the right of the esophagus. The nerve appears large and surrounded by a significant amount of inflammatory tissue, despite the patient's aggressive PPI management.

Posteriorly was in the problem. I thought there was going to be a lot of adhesions posteriorly due to the fact that he has an aortic stent there.
The surgeon navigates behind the left atrium, revealing a visible vessel adjacent to the esophagus, which is typically shortened due to esophageal stricture.

Kind of my heart stopped to slow down a little bit. Like, what is that? And it has clear fluid coming out of it. Did not know if I got into the pericardium or, you know, in further exploration, that was kind of a, uh, like a vessel. So I don't know if that was a lymphatic duct and had some fluids from there. So I decided to do a leak test, did an EGD.

The surgeon switches the robotic camera to near infrared mode to visualize the stomach and esophagus. The esophagus appears highly inflamed despite maximal treatment, but there is no visible evidence of a leak.

The surgeon closes the hiatus using Ethabon sutures with Pledgets. The anterior vagotomy provides sufficient intraabdominal esophageal length. The surgeon then proceeds to kocherize the duodenum, retracting it to the patient's right.

The surgeon performs co-ochorization, revealing the left inferior vena cava joining the inferior vena cava beneath the colon. The surgical field is in ICG mode, highlighting the common bile duct. Cold scissors are used to separate adhered tissues, which are stuck due to a previous perforation treated conservatively.

The surgeon identifies the location of the stricture externally and begins by opening the pylorus from the stomach side towards the duodenum. Upon encountering a pinhole, the surgeon proceeds to open the duodenum to connect both sides. A mass is identified at the site of the stricture, excised, and sent to pathology. The mass is visually assessed and suspected to be malignant, but frozen section analysis reveals it to be pancreatic tissue.

The surgeon re-approximates the mucosa using vial sutures after excising a mucosal mass approximately two by three centimeters in size. The stomach and duodenum portions are connected, ensuring no injury to the common bile duct. An EGD scope is passed down to the duodenum to confirm the integrity of the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a Heineken-Michels pyloroplasty, manipulating the pylorus to improve gastric outflow. The surgical field shows minimal blood loss despite the lengthy procedure.
